question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of vacant Housing Executive properties, requiring maintenance before they are allocated, within the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive carries out repair work on all dwellings going through the relet process. At a minimum, this will constitute health and safety checks with any further additional work necessary to bring the property to a lettable standard. As of 12th March 2026, there were 14 actionable* and 38 non-actionable** void dwellings in the Foyle Parliamentary Constituency.*Actionable voids are properties which are vacant while in the process of being allocated and include properties undergoing change of tenancy repairs and difficult to let properties. **Non-Actionable voids are properties that are not immediately available for allocation and are held vacant for operational reasons – for example, decanting purposes (where they are being used to house a tenant on a temporary basis pending works to their home), planned maintenance schemes and properties which have been identified for sale/transfer or have approval for demolition.”

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the reason why oil companies in Northern Ireland are not regulated, compared to those in the rest of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: Economic regulation of the heating oil market has never operated here, in Britain, or indeed anywhere in Europe. The Consumer Council actively monitors heating oil prices across the North and publishes weekly average prices. This provides consumers with clear, comparable information on prevailing prices, helping them to make informed purchasing decisions and to shop around between suppliers.  The Trading Standards Service works to protect consumers from illegal trading practices, such as unfair pricing, while supporting legitimate businesses. It provides guidance to oil retailers on their obligations and if consumers have a complaint about oil deliveries, they should contact the Department for the Economy’s consumer advice helpline Consumerline on 0300 123 6262 or log on to Consumerline.  I welcome the Competition and Markets Authority’s announcement of a market study into home heating oil. It will serve to strengthen competition, improve transparency and consumer protection, and ensure pricing reflects genuine costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any support available to businesses at risk of flooding in Foyle.
answer_text: While there are currently no specific support schemes available to farmers in Foyle whose land is at risk of flooding, I am continuing to monitor the situation.The Farm Sustainability Payment, as part of SAP, is designed to improve the resilience of farm businesses and help withstand ‘shocks’ that are beyond their ability to manage effectively and encouraging businesses to become more environmentally sustainable and efficient.I intend to bring forward a Crisis Framework as part of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP). This framework will provide a pre-defined approach for government intervention in the event of an identified significant crisis impacting the agricultural sector in Northern Ireland.The SAP is a strong example of climate adaptation action being progressed by my Department, as we work with and support our agricultural sector to be more resilient to the more frequent and severe impacts brought by climate change. Delivery of the SAP is a key action in the draft Northern Ireland’s third Climate Change Adaptation Programme (NICCAP3), which my Department has developed, with input from all Departments.As a cross-cutting Programme with more than 280 actions to build climate resilience, the NICCAP3 requires Executive agreement. I am currently engaging with my Executive colleagues to seek their agreement to finalise NICCAP3 and lay it in this Assembly as soon as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there are plans for more schemes in the Foyle constituency under the Housing for All Shared Housing Programme.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“At present, there are four Housing for All developments within the Foyle constituency that form part of the Housing for All Shared Housing Programme. These include the Ark Housing Association scheme at Tillie & Henderson, Abercorn Road, comprising 131 units, which was added to the programme in 2024/25. The four schemes are delivered by Apex, Radius, Choice and Ark Housing Associations, who work jointly on the fiveyear good relations plans for each development through the Foyle Advisory Group. There are no schemes from Foyle identified for inclusion in 2025/26 Housing for All Programme. In terms of future plans for the Foyle constituency, all potential Housing for All schemes must first be identified on the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP). Housing Associations nominate sites they wish to bring forward as shared developments, and any proposals in the Foyle area will be considered as part of the long list reviewed by the NIHE Strategic Oversight Group. This Group includes representatives from Housing for All, Regional Place Shaping Teams, the Development Programme Group, Community Cohesion, the Rural Regeneration Unit, Equality and Regional Management. From this process, a shortlist is agreed to maximise the impact of good relations support through the programme.The scale of delivery in 2026/27 and future years is dependent on the budget allocated annually. Subject to available resources, the programme aims to deliver around 200 shared social homes each year as part of the Social Housing Development Programme, reflecting the commitment set out in previous programmes for government.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools in the Foyle constituency that have availed of RAISE funding, in the last 24 months.
answer_text: The RAISE Programme consists of two key elements, Locality Led Projects and Cross Cutting Initiatives. A total of 61 successful locality projects have been identified following an open call, the process to complete and agree Letters of Offer is well advanced and needs to be completed prior to funding being released to the Locality Led Projects.  I can confirm that schools in the Foyle constituency will benefit from this element of the RAISE Programme, the total number of schools cannot be confirmed until Letters of Offer have been signed and returned to the Department by the lead organisation for each project. The RAISE Programme also includes a number of Cross Cutting Initiatives, of these, five schools in the Foyle constituency are participating in the Reading with AI Research Project, four schools participated in the Halloween Revision Scheme as part of the Holiday Revision Support Programme and nine schools from the Foyle Constituency have expressed an interest in participating in the Easter Revision Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on his engagement with the US Semiquincentennial Commission.
answer_text: The Commission’s recent visit to Northern Ireland was important in strengthening our historic connections to the United States in advance of the America250 semi-quincentennial celebrations in 2026.During the visit, the Commission visited Parliament Buildings and met with political representatives, including the Speaker, to highlight the importance of the relationships and to explore opportunities for future cooperation. I hosted a successful networking reception and dinner for the Commission in Hillsborough Castle involving senior representatives from across business, culture, tourism, heritage and education. The Commission also visited PRONI and the Ulster-Scots Agency - each highlighting Northern Ireland’s distinctive contributions to the American story.I was delighted to sign a Memorandum of Understanding with the Commission during a visit to the Ulster American Folk Park. This agreement marks the historic connections that exist, and the vital role played by the Ulster-Scots in shaping America.  This grants Northern Ireland “special status” with the Commission and positions us at the heart of the America250 commemorations.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any progress made in developing phase 2 of Urban Villages.
answer_text: The Urban Villages Initiative has had a transformative impact within local communities in Belfast and Derry. To date a total of £73.6 million has been invested in Urban Village areas.  £56 million has been invested in the capital programme with 59 schemes completed and £17.6 million revenue funding awarded. This work has, provided much-needed physical regeneration, creating safer communities, and making a positive difference to peoples’ lives.Building on this success, we are determined that the benefits of this scheme reach even more communities. Our efforts are focused on identifying areas that would gain most from the support and investment offered through the Urban Villages Initiative.Following a meeting with officials on 17th November, we are working to finalise the details of Phase 2 of the programme.Once complete, Members will receive a comprehensive update outlining the next steps and opportunities ahead.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how their Department plans to improve co-ordination between Departments and agencies, during storm responses.
answer_text: We recognise the critical importance of effective coordination between Departments and agencies during storm responses.The Executive Office Civil Contingencies Division is the custodian of the ‘Northern Ireland Civil Contingencies Framework – Building Resilience Together’ (NICCF) which embeds co-ordination and collaboration through a responsive and agile escalation on a cross-departmental and multi-agency basis. As part of NICCF, Departments, agencies and other partners meet regularly to ensure the efficient exchange of information. Our department is committed to learning from our responses and regularly engages in identifying lessons after exercises and severe storm events with all partners and agencies. We are taking forward the lessons identified from Storm Eowyn, via the Civil Contingencies Group (NI), which will improve co-ordination between Departments and agencies, and will also inform a review of the NICCF later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement he has had with the UK Government, in relation to the Local Growth Fund.
answer_text: I refer the Member to answers provided to previous questions AQW 31249/22-27, AQO 2452/22-27, AQO 2458/22-27 and AQW 32531/22-27.In addition, I attended a trilateral meeting with the Secretary of State, Hilary Benn, and Steve Reed, Secretary of State for Housing Communities and Local Government on 22 October. We have agreed to an intensive period of trilateral working, at official level, to urgently develop a way forward.I also wrote to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury on 23 October asking him to provide flexibility on the Capital / Resource split for the Local Growth Fund. I emphasised the negative impact the proposal for a Capital-heavy fund would have here, and in particular, for our Voluntary and Community Sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the amount of funding allocated to the Skills Action Plan
answer_text: The Skills Action Plan includes a range of commitments that span multiple financial years which will be subject to securing funding. A priority for the Department will be securing the necessary funding in the multi-year budget exercise announced by the Finance Minister. In this challenging financial environment, we are taking a proactive approach to secure additional funding from alternative sources, such as the Enhanced Investment Zones and Shared Island Fund. We have also been engaging closely with our counterparts in south of Ireland to maximise the potential from the PeacePlus funds.Last year through my Skills Fund, I invested £12m across 20 projects, which supported 10,000 individual and 500 employers, in a range of upskilling and reskilling programmes across the North.This year I have continued this support, allocating £9m to date, across projects like Skill Up, Union Learning Fund, Inclusive Futures and Step Up. Expectations are that 8,000 individuals will receive upskilling and reskilling training.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the action his Department is taking to address the reported maintenance backlog in schools to ensure pupil and staff safety.
answer_text: Significant work has been progressed by the Education Authority’s maintenance service to address inescapable statutory requirements, to keep schools open and safe and to provide additional specialist places for children with Special Educational Needs.  		The schools’ estate needs increased and sustained funding to address the many years of underinvestment, and I will continue to make the case for this additional funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how her Department will ensure that the £300,000 Community Wealth Building investment delivers long-term economic benefits in Londonderry,
answer_text: This funding, jointly provided by the Department for the Economy and the Department for Communities, is being directed to pilot initiatives in both Larne and the North West. Each of the two pilots will receive £150,000.The North West pilot is led by Development Trusts NI who will bring together Trademark Belfast, and Enterprise North West to deliver a programme of activities in the North West area. Trademark will focus on exploring worker buyouts for business succession and economic resilience, while Enterprise North West will connect stakeholders and highlight the benefits of Community Wealth Building. The pilot includes working to improve social value in procurement in the region and help local social enterprises access credit union finance.My Department will closely monitor and assess its impact on local economic outcomes. Lessons learned will inform future policy and funding decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) what powers are available to address street drinking in Londonderry; and (ii) whether legislative changes are being considered.
answer_text: (i) The Local Government Act (NI) 1972 provides councils with the power to make bye-laws prohibiting the consumption of alcoholic drinks in designated public places. Under the Justice Act (NI) 2011, the PSNI can address alcohol-related public order offences such as being drunk in a public place, via Fixed Penalty Notices. (ii) The responses to the joint DoJ and DfC public consultation on anti-social behaviour legislation, including these powers, were published on 4 April 2025 and suggested a need for legislative reform.  My Department is currently working with DoJ and councils to determine appropriate next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken by his Department to address winter pressures in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: My Department has taken a whole-system approach to planning for winter pressures across all Health and Social Care Trusts, including the Western Trust. The Overarching Winter Preparedness Plan 2025–26, published on 16 October 2025, outlines a series of measures designed to protect services, improve patient flow, and manage pressures across the system.In the Western Trust area, a range of actions are being implemented to improve patient flow, reduce avoidable admissions, and enhance discharge processes. These include:Expansion of Hospital at Home services, with direct referral pathways from primary care enabling patients to receive medical care in their own homes, avoiding the need for hospital admission.Strengthened collaboration with care homes, including agreed protocols to manage certain conditions within the care home setting, helping to prevent unnecessary hospital attendances.Increased capacity for patients with dementia and complex needs, including the re-designation of residential beds and the opening of additional nursing and dementia beds to support discharge and improve flow.Establishment of a Control Room to proactively manage patient movement across hospital and community services, alongside a Discharge Co-ordination Team focused on promoting earlier and weekend discharges for patients assessed as medically fit.To support these efforts, my Department has repurposed £10 million to fund targeted actions across the care pathway, from pre-hospital care through acute care and into the community. These measures are designed to supplement existing bed capacity and provide alternative pathways for patients, which are often more appropriate than inpatient admission.In addition, my Department has taken a number of steps to improve patient flow at Altnagelvin Area Hospital, including:Opening an enhanced minor injuries unit in March 2024, operating from 8:00am to 9:00pm, seven days per week. This Phone First-led service is expected to see 20,000 patients annually, reducing pressure on the emergency department;Enhancing the Ambulatory Care Unit, now operating 9:00am to 9:00pm, seven days per week, with capacity for 12,000 bookable appointments per year;Establishing a consultant-led Respiratory Hub, offering clinics two and a half days per week for up to 1,200 patients annually; and,Providing seven-day respiratory consultant cover for both the emergency department and inpatient referrals.Collectively, these actions aim to improve patient flow, reduce congestion in emergency departments, and enhance hospital resilience across the Western Trust during periods of peak demand.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of (a) housing development applications; and (b) planning approvals in the Foyle constituency, which are currently stalled due to a lack of wastewater treatment capacity; and (ii) the (a) short-term; and (b) medium-term, interventions that are are being planned by NI Water or her Department to unlock development in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:(a) (b) NI Water has two wastewater treatment works (WwTW) within the Foyle constituency,  Culmore WwTW and Donnybrewer WwTW, and both currently have wastewater treatment capacity. As a result, there are no housing development applications or planning approvals in the Foyle constituency, currently stalled due to a lack of capacity in the wastewater treatment works.(a) (b) As Culmore WwTW and Donnybrewer WwTW both currently have capacity at the works, no interventions at either of the wastewater treatment works are currently planned by NI Water to unlock wastewater treatment capacity for development in the Foyle constituency. I continue to do all that I can to enable the release of wastewater capacity constraints to support future housing and wider economic development. Following June monitoring, I was able to allocate £11.3m to NI Water, which will enable the company to take forward the construction of Skeoge wastewater pumping station, which will help to unlock capacity for 3,000 houses in the Derry area.  NI Water is already progressing with the Lenamore wastewater pumping station, which will work in conjunction with Skeoge, to provide the necessary wastewater infrastructure to facilitate new connections in the local area. When the additional funding that I secured in June monitoring is taken into consideration, NI Water is now receiving 90% of what it identified it could deliver this year, representing a significant level of investment in our water and wastewater infrastructure.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to improve (i) cycle routes; (ii) greenways; and (ii) the active travel infrastructure in Foyle.
answer_text: Delivery of future active travel routes within our towns and cities, including those in the Foyle constituency, will be prioritised primarily in line with the emerging Active Travel Delivery Plan.. Public consultation on the draft plan closed on 28 February 2025 and my officials are now considering the responses received with a view to presenting a final plan to me for consideration. I hope to be in a position to publish the final plan by the end of this year. In addition, my officials are currently developing a number of active travel schemes that are at various stages of the design process including Clooney Road, Campsie, Moss Road and Fern Road, Gaillagh.My Department also carries out many other activities for the benefit of pedestrians and cyclists and details of upcoming footway resurfacing and other pedestrian enhancements will be outlined in my department’s annual report to Derry City and Strabane District Council.Greenways are generally council led with my department providing grant funding up to 50% of the capital costs of approved projects. I acknowledge the positive work that has taken place in recent years through partnership working with the Council and I look forward to further progress being made to expand the significant greenway network which has been developed. I recently launched a Greenway Grant funding round inviting councils to apply for capital funding to support delivery of their greenway priorities, spanning the 2025-26 and 26-27 financial years and I look forward to considering applications to support councils in the delivery of  their greenway priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how his Department plans to address the loss of the Community Crisis Intervention Service in Londonderry, following his decision to no longer provide funding support.
answer_text: The Community Crisis Intervention Service (CCIS) has had an important role in supporting vulnerable people, especially out of hours. I fully acknowledge the scale of that contribution.CCIS was commissioned using monies allocated through the Mental Health Support Fund, which was a one-off three-year scheme launched in 2021 using unspent Covid-19 funds. With all resources allocated, that funding has ended as planned, and unfortunately, with our ongoing extreme budget challenges, no further funds could be identified. Via Extern, CCIS applied to the Core Grant Scheme for the 2025/26 funding round, but it was not among the 25 highest scoring applications to progress to stage 2 of the assessment process. In total, the Department received 259 applications totalling over £13m. The selection of the 25 highest scoring applications to progress to stage 2 of the assessment process was based solely on agreed scoring criteria, rather than a Departmental determination of the value of the applicants. Those who were unsuccessful, such as Extern, were encouraged to seek feedback on their application, which is intended to assist them in applying for future funding rounds. I will be launching the application process for 2026/27 core grant funding in the coming weeks. The high standard of applications for core grant has reinforced the valuable contribution that voluntary and community sector organisations make towards health and social care outcomes.Alternative crisis services remain available. The Western Trust’s Home Treatment Team offers crisis support, alongside GP out-of-hours provision, emergency departments and a range of community and voluntary sector partners, and Lifeline provides round-the-clock community assistance. Over the summer we saw the publication of the new Protect Life 2 Action and implementation plans, which will substantially enhance our efforts to reduce suicide and self-harm. While budgets remain a significant challenge I have reallocated around £300,000 of funding to year 1 of the Action Plan to promote awareness raising and prevention and to support local work in delivering the Protect Life Strategy Actions across Northern Ireland.Implementing the new regional crisis service is a commitment in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031. The design, development and testing of a regional Mental Health Crisis Service will be dependent upon availability of Mental Health Strategy funding, as set out in the Mental Health Strategy Funding Plan 2021-31.  A Regional Mental Health Liaison and Crisis Response Home Treatment Care Network has also been set up. The focus of the work of this network is to establish regionalisation of pathways, operating procedures, thresholds, documentation, information, training and equity. In adopting the Royal College of Psychiatry Standards, service improvements will be progressed to ensure timely mental health assessment of those presenting in need within Emergency Departments and to have a regionally equitable Crisis Resp

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what his Department has delivered for the people of Foyle since he took office in February 2024.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on all capital works planned for GP practices in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: My Department remains committed to supporting developments in primary care infrastructure and continues to engage with GP Practices in the Foyle area to scope options for additional accommodation requirements for core General Medical Services (GMS) alongside any potential increase in capacity for GP Training consultation rooms and accommodation to provide Physiotherapy, Mental Health and Social Work services as part of the multi-disciplinary teams (MDT) roll out.A number of projects have recently been completed, are under way or are planned in the Foyle constituency area to create additional capacity within GP practices.  These include:Foyleside Family Practice – SPPG are currently exploring options with the practice to create adequate space to accommodate MDT staff.  Subject to business case approval, capital works are anticipated to be carried out in the 2025/26 and 2026/27 financial years.Cityside Health and Care Centre – a business case is currently being developed by the Western HSC Trust with regards to the development of a new Cityside HCC which will potentially see the relocation of 5 GP practices to the new facility. I await clarity on the upcoming proposed capital budget, which along with my Department’s Capital Investment Pan, will identify when funding will be available to allow this project to move forward. The project will be subject to the usual business case approvals (to include any potential revenue costs as well as capital costs).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the resurfacing of Kinsale Park, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads within the Foyle area that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Derry and Strabane District Council.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections of the road network and repair any defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has conducted any recent assessments of the impact of university-related traffic on residential areas near Ulster University's Magee campus.
answer_text: My department has not carried out any recent assessments of university related traffic on the residential area near the Magee campus. However, as part of the planning process for the University expansion, it is likely that the applicant will be required to complete a traffic impact assessment to analyse the potential effect of the expansion proposals on the transport network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any discussions her Department has had with Ulster University regarding the provision of adequate parking infrastructure, to support the growing student and staff population at the Magee campus.
answer_text: Officials from my Department have engaged extensively with Ulster University and other key stakeholders, including the Magee Expansion Taskforce, to discuss infrastructure requirements associated with the growth of the Magee campus. One of the key issues raised during these engagements, by both students and members of the local community, has been the provision of adequate parking. Indeed, these concerns were formally presented by community representatives to the Taskforce in the lead-up to its Interim Report.In response, the Taskforce recommended that the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) work in partnership with Derry City and Strabane District Council (DCSDC), the PSNI, and other relevant agencies, to develop a comprehensive traffic management strategy for the area. This will include consideration of how best to support use of public and active travel, as well as car parking. 	We recognise concerns around parking as the campus grows. Integrated planning across transport, public realm, and capital development will ensure that this is taken into account as part of that broader approach including a shift toward more sustainable and inclusive travel options.	My Department will continue to work closely with Ulster University, DCSDC, DfI and other partners to ensure that transport planning is addressed as part of the strategic infrastructure planning required to support the successful expansion of the Magee campus.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the immediate steps her Department is taking to address the surge in illegal and inconsiderate parking in the Northland Avenue area, Londonderry, particularly in light of the Ulster University Magee campus expansion.
answer_text: The streets around Magee Campus receive regular parking enforcement patrols and Traffic Attendants take enforcement action against any vehicle parked in contravention of specified decriminalised parking restrictions or prohibitions. To coincide with the commencement of the new academic year, the frequency of these parking enforcement patrols has recently been increased in an attempt to address poor parking behaviour in the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the completion of the full A6 Londonderry to Dungiven Dualling scheme, including Phase 2, remains a flagship Executive commitment.
answer_text: I can confirm that the completion of the full A6 Londonderry to Dungiven Dualling scheme, including Phase 2, remains a flagship Executive commitment.However, part of the works for Phase 2 of the A6 Derry to Dungiven Flagship scheme, between Caw and Drumahoe, will encroach on the illegal waste site at Mobuoy. My Department has been working collaboratively with our colleagues in DAERA who have launched a public consultation on their Draft Optimum Remediation Strategy on 13 June 2025.  The consultation period closes on 2nd October 2025 and my officials are currently considering this document and will provide response to the consultation process as necessary.In order to progress Phase 2 of this major road infrastructure project, we must await finalisation of the DAERA strategy and the associated timeline for its implementation.  Once this is confirmed my officials can determine the most efficient delivery mechanism for the progression of the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) the progress made towards delivering a new Emergency Department at Altnagelvin Area Hospital; and (ii) how will this address the ongoing concerns about emergency care provision in the North West.
answer_text: Following the announcement of the outcome of the UK wide Spending Review 2025 in June, the Department of Finance commissioned a Budget exercise that will determine the capital budgets for Northern Ireland Department’s for the years 2026-27 to 2029-30.My ability to commit to funding capital projects, including Altnagelvin ED, will depend on my Department’s capital allocation from the Budget process. The draft Budget outcome is not expected to be confirmed until later this year and will inform the development of the Department of Health (DoH) capital investment plan which will set out the projects to be taken forward over the next ten years.The Western Trust has however secured funding for some works to the Emergency Department at Altnagelvin Hospital to improve patient/staff experience, these include the provision of additional toilet and shower facilities, additional vending area and an upgrade of the current staff kitchen/rest area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline how the new Enabling Access to Justice Reform Programme will improve support for victims in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: I launched the Enabling Access to Justice Reform Programme to improve accessibility and to ensure the justice system is fair, proportionate and responsive and work is very much focused on improving the experience and outcomes of those who come into contact with the system, and victims and other vulnerable citizens in particular. The Programme aims to minimise acrimony, stress and trauma for those who come into contact with the justice system by enabling disputes to be resolved at the earliest possible juncture and by ensuring the fullest range of supports are available at the earliest possible opportunity through comprehensive delivery models targeted at specific groups such as victims of domestic abuse. I also propose to examine scope and widen eligibility for legal aid to ensure advice and representation is available to those who need it most and that the system is not weaponised to perpetrate further abuse.  Collectively reforms should ensure citizens across Northern Ireland, including in the Foyle constituency, can effectively participate in proceedings and that the long-term impacts and costs they can experience are mitigated.I consulted on the Programme Delivery Plan earlier this year. I am currently considering responses and expect to publish an updated Delivery Plan shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the establishment of the Local Economic Partnership in Londonderry and Strabane.
answer_text: The Local Economic Partnership in Derry City and Strabane Council held its first meeting in April 2025. It has since met several times and has made excellent progress. A draft action plan has been developed, and Council officials are actively working with Department officials towards the finalisation of this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) an update on the implementation of the £10 million North West Regeneration Fund; and (ii) how this investment will ensure that regeneration benefits are felt by local communities, particularly in the Historic City Conservation Area.
answer_text: My Department continues to engage with the Londonderry Inner City Trust (ICT), as our delivery partner for the North West Regeneration Fund.  Since the announcement of the Fund in June 2025, officials have been working with the ICT to develop project business cases for key strategic projects located within the Historic City Conservation area. It is envisaged that investment through the North West Regeneration Fund will also help to unlock access to further funding from other sources such as the National Lottery Heritage Fund and the new UK Government Neighbourhood Fund. Together these funding packages will contribute to placemaking within the historic city centre, and act as a lever for further private and public sector investments in the area. It is also expected that the focus on the regeneration and conservation of strategic buildings such as built heritage assets will help to increase footfall in the city and create additional employment opportunities that will further benefit the local community.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any targeted interventions being developed to address the widening health inequality gaps in Londonderry and Strabane, particularly in relation to drug and alcohol related admissions, which are significantly above the regional average.
answer_text: Making Life Better (MLB) is the overarching strategic framework for public health through which the Executive committed to creating the conditions for individuals, families and communities to take greater control over their lives and be enabled to lead healthy lives. MLB is underpinned by a range of public health strategies and policies which focus on specific issues, including substance use.Across the North West area, the PHA works with a range of partners on health and wellbeing interventions/programmes, including Derry City and Strabane District Council, Fermanagh and Omagh District Council, other NICS Departments, HSC Trusts, Primary Care, Local Government, Voluntary and Community Sector, local businesses and many other partners to promote activities that will encourage and enable healthier lifestyles that contribute to better health outcomes.My Department continues to lead on the implementation of the cross-departmental strategy to prevent and reduce the harm related to substance use in Northern Ireland. The strategy includes measures to reduce the availability and accessibility of alcohol and other substances and requires a whole system approach. I am committed to working across the NI Executive, with colleagues across the UK and in Ireland to tackle these issues and the underlying societal causes.It is vital that people understand the harm that alcohol can cause. Therefore, my Department and the Public Health Agency promote the UK Chief Medical Officers’ low risk guidelines, which state that to drink at low risk, men and women should drink no more than 14 units of alcohol a week. We also promote specific awareness-raising events such as Dry January and Alcohol Awareness Week.Sadly, figures confirm that alcohol related harms continue to be felt by some parts of our community more than others, with the percentage of alcohol-specific deaths in our most deprived areas (37.6%) almost four times that of the least deprived areas (9.8%).It is because of the unacceptable levels of harm and health inequalities caused by alcohol that I have indicated my intention, subject to Executive and Assembly agreement, to bring forward legislation to introduce Minimum Unit Pricing (MUP) of Alcohol in Northern Ireland.In addition, we are working with Cooperation and Working Together (CAWT) on the rollout of the Community Alcohol Detox+ (CAD+) Project to provide services to people experiencing alcohol dependence syndrome, and their families / carers. This is funded via PeacePlus.I have also introduced a targeted, place-based approach called Live Better which has been designed to help address health inequalities by bringing targeted health support to communities which need it most. Live Better was tested in Derry/Londonderry (Fountain, Bogside, Brandywell and Creggan) and is currently being evaluated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of reported (a) physical; and (b) verbal assaults, against Northern Ireland Ambulance Service staff, in each of the last five years; and (ii) what specific measures his Department is taking to protect ambulance personnel and deter future attacks.
answer_text: The number of physical and verbal assaults against Northern Ireland Ambulance Service staff, in each of the last five years is shown in the table below*.Financial YearReported Incidents of Physical Abuse Reported Incidents of Verbal AbuseTotal Reported Incidents 2020-213402716112021-223913036942022-233812746552023-244572657222024-25367                230                  597*Please note: The information in the table above was requested from and provided by NIAS for the purpose of this response.My Department and NIAS are both committed to doing everything possible to keep staff safe. The high incidence of violence and aggression towards staff is recorded on the Trust’s Corporate Risk Register and in response, NIAS has introduced a range of measures to help protect staff physical safety and well-being.This includes provision of body-worn cameras which are available to paramedics attending calls. These act as a potential deterrent and allow staff to capture video footage of instances of violence and aggression.NIAS has a dedicated Violence Reduction Officer in post whose role is to raise awareness about the risks and ensure that appropriate steps are taken following an incident, including that staff have access to psychological support. NIAS has also established an internal multi-professional working group to monitor and review instances of violence and aggression and to develop policies and interventions to try to mitigate the risks and further protect staff.In addition, NIAS works closely with the Police Service of Northern Ireland to ensure that they are provided with evidence to support criminal investigation and enforcement against individuals who perpetrate violence towards its staff. The Trust also participates in national working groups to draw on best-practice and approaches taken by ambulance services in England, Scotland and Wales to reduce violence and aggression via the Association of Ambulance Chief Executives (AACEs).

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the current number of vacancies on the board of Invest NI; and (ii) when she plans to fill those vacancies.
answer_text: There is one vacancy on the Board of Invest NI following a recent resignation.  This vacancy is included as part of my Department’s succession planning for the recruitment of up to five new Board members to replace those members whose second terms will end on 31 March 2026.A public appointment competition launched in October and the appointments will be effective from 1 April 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the implementation of the Living With Water Programme for Londonderry, including details of any (i) planned or ongoing capital works; and (ii) timelines for delivery.
answer_text: My Department published the draft Living With Water in Derry Plan for a 12-week public consultation on Monday 13 January 2025. This strategic draft plan details drainage and wastewater issues alongside potential opportunities for solutions to these issues.  It promotes the use of nature-based drainage and flood management solutions through collaborative working.  I intend to publish a consultation report and the final Living With Water in Derry Plan by the end of 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any steps his Department is taking to ensure that schools are equipped to both (i) teach; and (ii) responsibly integrate, artificial intelligence technologies into the classroom.
answer_text: My Department is currently working with a range of education partners to develop guidance for schools on the use of Artificial Intelligence (AI). The guidance will promote the safe and responsible use of AI.  The guidance highlights the opportunities which AI presents, including skills development at teacher and pupil levels and is an enabling tool for workload management, planning and teaching. My intention is to provide guidance to schools which will be informed by consultation with education professionals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the road safety measures, agreed for the Fountain Estate and Carlisle Road, Londonderry, following a meeting with her officials, will be implemented.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on progress on the restoration work at the former Austin's building, Londonderry, following the £1.2 million grant awarded by his Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement he plans to have with the Treasury to ensure that Northern Ireland receives its fair share of the Defence Growth Deal.
answer_text: The growth deal is a matter for the British Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of funding his Department has provided for jobs fairs in the Foyle constituency, over the past three years.
answer_text: Since April 2023 my department has delivered 19 local, regional, sector specific, cross-border and virtual job fairs in the Foyle Constituency.Costs cannot be separated out for these events as some incur no expenditure, whilst others are funded from multiple sources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps her Department is taking to ensure the aerospace and defence sector has a pipeline of skilled workers, from apprentices to engineers.
answer_text: My Department recognises the importance of ensuring sectors across our economy have a pipeline of skilled workers - increasing our productivity and widening access to good jobs.  The Department funds and facilitates a wide range of provision and engagement to support this aim. The ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeships programmes support talent development across a wide range of occupational areas, from Level 2 and Level 3 apprenticeships through to higher level qualifications.  These include apprenticeships in areas such as engineering, mechatronics, logistics, ICT and procurement and supply.Both apprenticeship programmes are underpinned by a network of 18 employer-led Sectoral Partnerships, including the Advanced Manufacturing and Engineering Sectoral Partnership, which help ensure that apprenticeship training remains aligned with industry standards and responsive to technological advancements. These partnerships work closely with employers and qualification bodies to shape and refresh apprenticeships based on real-time labour market demand.The Skill Up 2025/26 programme offers fully funded courses in key growth areas such as advanced manufacturing, software development, and cyber security, with qualifications from Level 2 to Level 7. The programme is designed to support people in skilling, reskilling, or upskilling in response to technological innovation, emerging industries, and evolving job market demands. The Skills Focus programme supports SMEs with fewer than 250 employees to upskill staff from Levels 2 to 7, while Assured Skills Academies are demand-led, pre-employment training programmes that equip individuals with the skills needed to compete for guaranteed job vacancies with new or expanding businesses, including those supported by Foreign Direct Investment. My Department’s Careers Service also has a dedicated Careers Occupational Information Unit, which tracks developments across local sectors and produces careers information resources that aim to raise awareness of careers opportunities available locally. Careers publications and labour market update articles are available in STEM related subjects, including Engineering and Advanced Manufacturing; Maths; and Physics.Our universities also play a critical role in developing the highly skilled talent required by industry. From apprenticeships to advanced engineering qualifications, they are equipping individuals with the expertise needed to support innovation and growth. To ensure higher education provision is closely aligned with labour market demand, there are established Outcome Agreements with each university, setting out shared commitments to deliver skills that meet the current and future needs of the local economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she plans to remove the limited services policy, still operating in DfI Roads - Western Division.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British GovernmentIn response, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network. Until such times as the funding for road maintenance can be stabilised, it is not possible to determine when my department could return to normal Essential Maintenance Service levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department is engaging directly with NI Water to seek solutions to address water infrastructure capacity concerns, potentially affecting the expansion of the Ulster University Magee campus.
answer_text: The Magee Expansion Taskforce invited officials from NI Water to provide a briefing on the current and projected capacity of water infrastructure in the city of Derry. During the meeting held on 22 November 2024, NI Water officials presented on several key issues, including support for planning applications and the shortfall in unfunded wastewater recovery infrastructure. The minutes of the meeting note their confirmation that, unlike many other parts of the north, the city appeared to have sufficient capacity to accommodate some development. The minutes of this meeting are available under UU Magee Taskforce - meeting minutes 22 November 2024.My officials will continue to engage with NI Water to assess any existing or emerging issues that may impact the city’s water infrastructure and, by extension, the proposed expansion of the Magee campus.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on discussions to transfer the management of Ebrington Square to Derry City and Strabane District Council.
answer_text: Following a meeting in May 2025 between Derry City and Strabane District Council and The Executive Office (TEO), it was agreed to draft three proposed service level agreements (SLAs) related to Ebrington. The proposed SLAs cover the following areas:Site cleaning and maintenance;Site management and car parking; andEvents, animation, and site promotion.Since that meeting, TEO officials and council officers have collaborated to define the scope of work and budget for the site cleaning and maintenance SLA, which is expected to be approved in October 2025.Work is ongoing to draft the remaining two SLAs, and it is anticipated that these will be finalised and agreed in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on progress to address traffic congestion at the Caw Roundabout, Londonderry.
answer_text: Following detailed considerations, I am advised that my officials have given approval to the alternative mitigation strategy put forward by the developer, South Bank Square. The alternative proposals will see the Caw Roundabout upgrade delivered sooner, with the works to be in place prior to occupation of more than 262 dwellings. In addition, the upgrade is time-bound and must be completed prior to the end of November 2026.My officials continue to work with the developer to progress the detailed design of the Caw Roundabout upgrade. The preliminary design and design parameters have been agreed and it is anticipated that the detailed design will be agreed later this year, to enable the works to commence on site in early 2026.This should provide a significant improvement in both road safety and traffic progression on the road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the road safety measures agreed for Victoria Road, Magheramason, will be installed.
answer_text: Advance warning signs on both approaches to the controlled crossing on Victoria Road, Magheramason, have been proposed.  The warning signs, along with SLOW road markings adjacent to the new signs will be located approximately 100 metres in advance of the existing controlled crossing.  The warning signs will be placed on a high visibility backing board and the existing traffic island bollards will be replaced with new high visibility bollards.An order for the manufacture of these signs has been issued and when delivered the installation will be programmed along with the associated road markings. This is anticipated to begin in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any role her Department has in the provision of comfort animals for offenders at Magilligan Prison; and (ii) her assessment of this provision.
answer_text: ANSWER:The use of emotional support animals by the Northern Ireland Prison Service is an operational matter for the relevant Governors in Charge of each prison establishment.It is widely recognised that the use of emotional support dogs can offer significant therapeutic benefits. They are currently utilised across the justice system, including for victims, and in supporting children, young people and their loved ones before they give evidence in court.In all prison establishments, dogs offer support to people with poor mental health and addiction issues, help promote positive changes in behaviour and reduce stress and anxiety.  Support dogs can also help prisoners enhance their social skills, self-esteem, and self-confidence.  These benefits can contribute to reducing the risk that someone might re-offend and prevent further victims of crime in the future.A recent newspaper article, relying on an unnamed source, made a number of inaccurate and unfounded claims regarding the care of Bailey, the support dog at Magilligan Prison.  Bailey is registered with a local veterinary practice and is in good health.  Local council officials were also recently invited to visit Magilligan Prison and confirmed that Bailey is in excellent condition, appropriately housed and properly safeguarded.The Prison Service takes its duty of care for animals’ safety and wellbeing very seriously and has an established record of working with a range of animal charities to offer animals both a caring home and a purposeful life.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the funding her Department is providing for road safety awareness in the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: Road safety remains a top priority, and I am committed to ensuring that everyone who uses our roads can do so safely. That is why I have reinstated the road safety advertising budget and launched a new Road Safety Strategy aimed at keeping communities safe while using our roads.Following careful consideration for my Department, I have approved an allocation £1,946,000 to Road Safety Promotion in the 2025/26 financial year. This investment reflects our ongoing commitment to saving lives and reducing harm on our roads.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department is taking to address the sewerage capacity issues affecting planning applications in Londonderry, including the teaching block at Ulster University Magee Campus.
answer_text: NI Water has advised that there has been no change to the wastewater capacity position, in the immediate area around Magee Campus.  This area is open to new wastewater connections; however, NI Water has advised that a developer funded intervention may still be required. The detail of which will worked through as part of NI Water’s pre planning application process, as is normal business.  NI Water has continually stressed the importance of submitting Pre Development Enquiry applications, as soon as possible for all known developments under this project, at all meetings and working groups regarding Magee Campus. NI Water will continue to work with the Ulster University, to ensure that it can deliver the proposed expansion at the Magee Campus.To help address wastewater capacity issues and encourage economic growth for all parts of the North, I will continue to develop the three-pronged approach that is already in place, which is working with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment for NI Water, secondly, progressing my  Water, Sustainable Drainage and Flood Management Bill, and thirdly, considering options to introduce developer contributions to help fund wastewater infrastructure improvements.  Following June Monitoring I allocated £11million to NI Water to unlock 3,000 houses in the Derry area. Since my predecessor John O’Dowd and I came into office we have provided ringfenced allocations to enable the unlocking of wastewater capacity for over 5,000 properties across the North. This is already above the 4,300 that NI Water had originally planned to connect, if fully funded, by the end of this Price Control Period in 2028, and it is only 2025.Additionally, I have consistently said that innovative thinking and working in partnership hold the key to unlocking our wastewater constraints. The recent announcement connecting 400 properties in Newry shows what can be achieved when we challenge traditional thinking and focus on smarter, more sustainable solutions – this lies at the heart of my three-pronged approach.In my 25/26 Budget, NI Water will be provided with over £500m of public money to enable it to deliver essential water and sewerage services for all people across the North. When the additional funding that I secured in June monitoring is taken into consideration, NI Water is now receiving 90% of the capital budget that it had identified it could deliver this year, representing a significant level of investment in our water and wastewater infrastructure. I will continue to do all that I can to enable the release of wastewater capacity constraints to support future housing and wider economic development. Furthermore,  my department established a Housing Supply Cross-Departmental group  involving officials from my Department, the Department for Communities, NI Water and the Housing Executive, to help unlock wastewater capacity to enable housing connections.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) number; and (ii) location of road resurfacing schemes, completed in the Foyle constituency in the last 12 months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any regeneration schemes planned for the Foyle constituency in the next five years.
answer_text: The Department has several regeneration projects currently ongoing or planned for the Foyle constituency over the next five years, as outlined below:I recently announced the North West Regeneration Fund and can confirm that my Department have begun working with the Londonderry Inner City Trust, to identify suitable regeneration projects.  The Fund makes available up to £10 million Financial Transactions Capital to support regeneration interventions over the next five years.Work to regenerate the Fort George site is ongoing and my Department continues to work alongside the Western Health & Social Care Trust regarding a Full Planning Application for a proposed health and care centre.Work on the £5 million Inner Walled City Public Realm scheme is continuing and is expected to be completed in the summer of 2026. DfC has also provided funding to develop design proposals for the revitalisation of the historic Cathedral Quarter in Londonderry.    These works are in addition to the proposed commencement of the City Deal programme that includes the £13.4 million Walled City Regeneration project which is programmed for completion in 2032.

I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:		‘Footfall figures for libraries in the Foyle Constituency for the last five years are detailed below:Library2020/21 2021/22 2022/232023/24 2024/25 Creggan 4,44421,59916,556 39,325Derry Central 63,085149,46197,117Shantallow 18,92754,53770,143Strathfoyle 5,11014,70630,41931,011Waterside 9,84822,63052,94754,084Notes:2020/21 figures are from 21 July 2020 to 31 March 2021. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current patient numbers within (a) nursing; and (b) care homes in the Foyle constituency; and (ii) the capacity of each.
answer_text: There are 468 registered Care Homes in Northern Ireland, providing a total of 15,836 beds. The Western and Southern Trust areas have the least number of registered care home beds, in comparison to the other Trusts. The Western Health and Social Care Trust has 31 nursing homes and 36 residential care homes with a total of 2079 care home beds. (Census of Bed Availability in Registered Care Homes in Northern Ireland on 27 September 2023 (Dec 2023)).The Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT) has confirmed that the Foyle constituency falls within the BT47 and BT48 postcode areas.Within these postcode areas the following numbers of registered care home beds are available:Residential Care Homes: 151 registered bedsNursing Homes: 452 registered bedsThese figures are made up of beds from both the statutory and independent sectors.The WHSCT has advised that it can provide capacity figures for the current residents in bed for statutory facilities only.  WHSCT only operates residential care homes within these postcodes. Of the 59 Trust-operated residential care home beds, 58 are currently occupied. The Trust does not own or operate any nursing homes in the Foyle constituency. All other nursing and residential care homes in the area are privately owned. As such, the Trust does not hold current occupancy data for these facilities. The Department of Health acknowledges the need to increase bed availability and to make better use of existing vacant capacity across the region. Workstream 1 of the Social Care Collaborative Forum is taking forward work to progress a regional digital solution for bed capacity and availability. It is hoped that this digital solution will improve communication regarding location of available beds and provide an approach for data gathering that supports better identification, individual care planning and provision of services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any land that the Housing Executive has deemed as surplus to requirements, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive has confirmed that there are no sites in the Foyle constituency which have been deemed surplus to requirements”.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the last time her Department undertook river maintenance on the Faughan River, Foyle, to improve water flow; (ii) the total cost of the works; and (iii) whether there are any works planned for this river.
answer_text: Officials from my Department’s Rivers Directorate carry out scheduled maintenance on the Faughan River on a cyclical basis. Rural reaches are inspected and maintained every 5 years with the last inspection taking place in December 2023 with the subsequent maintenance works completed in September 2024. Urban reaches in Drumahoe are inspected and maintained annually.In addition, my Department also remove trees and debris from the river after storm events. In the past 12 months trees have been removed in July 2024, September 2024, November 2024, December 2024 and a large number of fallen trees were cleared after Storm Eowyn in January of this year.My officials estimate that £265k has been spent on watercourse maintenance on this reach of the River Faughn over the past 5 years. The next watercourse inspection on this reach of the River Faughan is due in the 2028/2029 financial year.My officials are currently considering a request to lower a gravel shoal near Cloghole and repairs to an earth flood defence near the Faughan Bridge, Maydown.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the (i) financial support; and (ii) administrative support provided by his Department to Derry City and Strabane District Council to meet the 70 per cent recycling target, in each year since the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 came into force.
answer_text: (i)	My Department offers financial assistance to local councils to transform kerbside recycling, infrastructure and services through the Household Waste Collaborative Change Programme (HWRCCP). While the programme offers up to 50% match funding for successful applications, no financial assistance has been provided to Derry City and Strabane District Council since the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 came into force.(ii)	DAERA has a strong record of engagement with all eleven local councils to ensure we are well-equipped to meet the ambitious recycling targets set in legislation.  Officials recently collaborated with Derry City and Strabane District Council to model a range of potential waste collection scenarios, designed to optimise waste collections and enhance recycling across the district. My Department provides funding to the Waste & Resources Action Programme (WRAP) to provide essential local council support, including specialist technical guidance and the development of the necessary evidence base for implementing change. The programme also includes collaborative efforts with local councils on key national and regional behaviour change campaigns through the National Communications Advisory Panel (NCAP), for example Recycle Week and Food Waste Action Week, designed to enhance the quality and quantity of recycling.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the total number of unoccupied (i) social homes and (ii) affordable homes in the Foyle constituency, as at June 2025
answer_text: The Department does not hold the information requested in relation to Registered Housing Associations.  In relation to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, this issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:‘As of 13th June 2025, the Housing Executive had 14 actionable* and 40 non-actionable** void properties in the Foyle constituency.*Actionable voids are properties which are vacant while in the process of being allocated/ awaiting change of tenancy repairs. These may also include properties deemed difficult to let. **Non-Actionable voids are properties that are not immediately available for allocation and are held vacant for operational reasons i.e. decanting purposes (where they are being used to house a tenant on a temporary basis pending works to their home), planned maintenance schemes, and properties which have been identified for sale/transfer or have approval for demolition’For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline how often major roads are maintained to ensure drains are not blocked by debris, to alleviate flooding, for example, the A6, A8, M22, M2 and M1.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial position for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. In response, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network. In recent years budgets are such that my Department can only aim to inspect and where necessary clean all gullies, once annually.On roads maintained under Design Build Finance Operate contracts, gullies are cleaned once per year on rural roads or twice per year on urban roads. Some key routes such as A12 and M3 receive a full clean of gullies, catchpits and channels, including jetting of carrier pipes three times per year.Additionally, my officials advise that when severe weather warnings are received, gully emptiers are placed on standby, stocks of sandbags, pumps and signs are checked and replenished and gullies and grilles at known flooding hotspots are inspected and when appropriate cleaned or debris cleared.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline what steps her Department is taking to address speeding issues on the Victoria Road, Magheramason.
answer_text: I am aware that local officials have attended a site meeting with elected representatives to hear residents’ concerns on speeding issues on the A5 Victoria Road in Magheramason. Following this meeting it is proposed to install advanced warning signs on both approaches to the controlled crossing adjacent to the petrol station. The warning signs, along with SLOW roads markings adjacent to the new signs will be located approximately 100 metres in advance of the controlled crossing.  The warning signs will be placed on a high visibility backing board and the existing traffic island bollards will be replaced with new high visibility bollards. I can advise that enforcement of speed limits is the responsibility of the PSNI. Incidents of speeding or inappropriate driver behaviour along the Victoria Road should be reported directly to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any scoping works have been carried out by her Department to extend the cycle path from Drumahoe to Altnagelvin Hospital.
answer_text: My Department has developed a draft Active Travel Delivery Plan including network maps for our larger towns and cities. The extension of the footway and cycleway at this location was included in the draft plan published for consultation.Public consultation on the draft plan, including consultation on the Department’s draft priority routes within the plan, closed on 28 February 2025. My officials are currently reviewing the responses received with a view to presenting a final plan to me for consideration in the autumn.I plan to give careful consideration to all feedback before publishing the plan later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on the Inner Walled City Public Realm project.
To ask the Minister for Communities, given the success of the JobStart 50+ Scheme, whether his Department has any plans to continue to run this programme.
answer_text: The JobStart 50+ Scheme was funded by HM Treasury’s Labour Market Evaluations and Pilots fund (LMEP) as a test and learn pilot scheme. Unfortunately, budget for this Treasury funded pilot programme has not been recurrent in 2025-26. I am in the process of agreeing my Department’s 2025-26 Budget allocations. In allocating my Budget, one of my priorities is employment support, to help people overcome barriers faced in moving into, retaining and progressing in employment.  My Budget allocation plans will be announced in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his analysis of the JobStart 50+ Scheme.
answer_text: Funding for the JobStart 50+ pilot allowed 180 participants to join the scheme with 160 completing their opportunity. I am pleased to advise that 83 (52%) participants moved into sustained employment either with their JobStart employer or with an alternative employer following their participation on the Scheme.  An independent evaluation is underway that includes a measurement of sustained employment 13 weeks after participants exit the JobStart Scheme. All participants exited the scheme by 31 March 2025 and the final evaluation is expected by 31 July 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the £61 million allocated for the expansion of the Multi-Disciplinary Team Programme, to detail (i) the amount that will be allocated to support the roll-out in the Foyle constituency; and (ii) the amount that will be distributed across local GP practices.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has considered reviewing the penalties for drivers who exceed speed limits.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.I have asked my officials for advice on reviewing the penalties for drivers who exceed speed limits, which has the potential to improve attitudes to speeding and would also need to be subject to a public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide an update on any steps her Department is taking to address child criminal exploitation in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 10 – AQO 1924/22-27ANSWERI, together with the Ministers of Health and Education launched the Child Criminal Exploitation definition and two-year Action Plan for Northern Ireland in September 2024. Through this collaborative Programme of work, led by the Safeguarding Board for NI, we are making steady progress on developing a whole system, cross-sectoral approach to the exploitation of our young people, which includes more effective identification, partnership working and problem solving, training for staff, and early intervention and prevention.My Department has also commissioned research to explore the barriers to identifying CCE in NI within the context of Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking to further inform policy and guidance.I am also currently engaging with the Home Office with a view to extending particular offences in the Crime and Policing Bill to Northern Ireland, through a Legislative Consent Motion which will be subject to Assembly approval. This includes an offence of child criminal exploitation and associated matters

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of trainee colleges in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Apprenticeship provision is delivered through two programmes, ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher-Level Apprenticeships (HLAs).ApprenticeshipsNI training is delivered by the six Further Education Colleges in addition to the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) and over twenty Private Training Organisations.  HLAs are delivered by all FE Colleges, in addition to CAFRE and the three Higher Education Institutions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how much of the funding earmarked for housing, announced by the Chancellor, will be spent in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: When the 2025/26 budget is confirmed, Northern Ireland Housing Executive, as the commissioning body for the programme, will have to consider how the budget may best be used to meet identified housing need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many of the 600 houses proposed to be purchased by the Housing Executive for temporary accommodation will be in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.The planned acquisition of 600 properties will be focused on reducing NIHE’s current exposure to high-cost temporary accommodation (particularly hotels and B&Bs).While the final locations have yet to be determined the principal considerations will include:Geographical areas where exposure to high-cost temporary accommodation is most prevalent.The nature of the temporary accommodation client groups (single people, couples, families).The availability of suitable properties to purchase that align with the above criteria.Value for money considerations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline his assessment of the Chancellor's Spring Statement 2025.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the number of applicants from the Foyle constituency that have been successful in securing the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment.
answer_text: The Victims’ Payments Board, which makes decisions on any entitlement to payment through the Scheme, has advised that its system does not currently allow for a breakdown of successful applications by constituency.  Inclusion of post code information in applications is not mandatory and therefore a manual review of more than 10,000 applications would be required to retrieve this information. As of week commencing 24 March 2025, there have been 1,993 successful determinations resulting in over £83 million paid to eligible applicants since the Scheme opened for applications.  1,031 determinations have been unsuccessful.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on all capital works planned for GP practices in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: A number of projects have recently been completed, are under way or are planned in the Foyle constituency area to create additional capacity within GP practices.  These include:Foyleside Family Practice – SPPG are currently reviewing options with the practice with regards to creating sufficient space to accommodate MDT staff.  Subject to business case approval, it is anticipated that capital works are likely to be undertaken in the 2025/26 financial year.Cityside Health and Care Centre – a business case is currently being developed by the Western HSC Trust with regards to the development of a new Cityside HCC which will potentially see the relocation of 5 GP practices to the new facility. Timing will be dependent on the outcome of the business case process and approval of funding via the Department’s overarching future capital plan.Capital works have been undertaken in 14 practices within the Foyle Constituency over the past 5 years under the Department’s Primary Care Infrastructure Development (PCID) programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when her Department plans to resurface the road network in Lincoln Courts estate, Londonderry, which is currently in serious disrepair.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy
