question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any support their Department can provide to the Royal Scottish Pipe Band Association NI to bring pipe band competitions to Ebrington, Londonderry.
answer_text: In addition to providing a licence for use of the Ebrington venue, the Department will consider additional support for events of civic benefit to the city.Pipe band competitions are non-profit making events which often attract considerable numbers of people, in particular ‘out of town’ visitors.   Such events therefore offer an opportunity to showcase Ebrington and the city to a wider tourism market.The Department is currently in discussion with the Royal Scottish Pipe Band Association NI Branch, including an offer of some financial support of £25,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on his bid to the Public Service Transformation Board for funding to support post-19 skills and education provision for young people leaving special schools and specialist provision in mainstream schools.
answer_text: My Department’s bid for funding to support Special Educational Leavers from Special Schools and Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools has progressed to stage two of the process.  My officials will meet with the interim Public Sector Transformation Board on Wednesday 18 September to hear their feedback directly in advance of the next stage of the process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department's resurfacing programme for the 2024-25 financial year in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: My Department is obliged to consult with local councils prior to the publication of our works programme.  A full list of resurfacing schemes for the Foyle constituency will be published in the upcoming DfI Roads Annual Report to Derry City and Strabane District Council which will be circulated to all elected representatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the time frame for the installation of additional road safety measures outside Ebrington Primary School, Nelson Drive, Londonderry.
answer_text: Following your meeting in September 2023 with local officials at Ebrington Primary School, a commitment was given to providing a number of safety measures including road markings to restrict parking at the access, and an improved pedestrian crossing point.I can confirm that the raised table pedestrian crossing point has recently been constructed and that the road markings to restrict parking at the access have been added to the road marking programme for delivery in the coming months.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Drumahoe, Ivy Mead, flood alleviation scheme.
answer_text: Development of potential flood alleviation proposals for Drumahoe is complex and remains ongoing. An economically viable scheme has been identified at this stage and consultants have been instructed to progress the detailed design phase of this project. It is hoped that the detailed design phase could be completed by early 2026. Following this, progress to procurement and ultimately work on site in 2027, will be dependent on proposals remaining viable and availability of capital funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the baby loss certificate scheme.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the response issued for AQW 14853 22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement she has had with the UK government in relation to the need for additional funding for the PSNI.
answer_text: I have consistently raised the issue of the underfunding of the justice system, including the need for more investment in policing, with successive Secretaries of State and Prime Ministers.  It was agreed at a meeting of the Executive in August that the First Minister and deputy First Minister, the Minister of Finance and I will write jointly to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury and the Secretary of State seeking additional funding to cover the costs incurred of policing the recent riots and disorder.  This letter remains with the First Minister and deputy First Minister and the Minister of Finance for consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the work of the interim Regional Planning Commission.
answer_text: The interim Regional Planning Commission, which was established in early 2023, continue to provide advice and support to the wider Planning Improvement Programme and are currently working together to identify solutions which will help the planning system here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the plans by the UK government to end the VAT exemption and business rates relief for private schools will apply to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: VAT is an Excepted Matter and responsibility for any changes in VAT, including ending exemptions for private schools, is a matter determined by the Treasury.The changes, announced on 29 July 2024, will apply here from 1 January 2025 onwards. Business rates are a Devolved Matter and provision in any Westminster Bill would not apply here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps his Department is taking to maximise the economic impact of The Open being in held at Royal Portrush Golf Club in 2025.
answer_text: Tourism NI, on behalf of my department, is committed to working with key partners to deliver benefits from The 153rd Open in 2025 in excess of those of The 148th Open in 2019. The previous event generated more than £100 million of combined economic and media benefit for the north, has supported the sector in achieving a record breaking £1.2 billion economic value by 2023 and golf tourism generating £68.2 million per year.The Open in 2025 will bring hundreds of thousands of visitors and will showcase the destination through the huge global broadcast reach and PR coverage generated, that will deliver a legacy for years to come.An industry engagement programme will be delivered in the lead up to the event, in partnership with local authorities. This will assist and advise traders and businesses on how best to avail of opportunities that The Open will present, including providing reasons for event visitors to extend their stay and explore the wider area, alongside the ongoing support for the development of new and immersive authentic experiences that deliver on the 'Embrace a Giant Spirit' brand.Sustainable workforce opportunities will be developed for beyond July 2025, through skills development, employment before, during and after the event, and volunteering. Discussions have begun with Derry and Strabane Labour Market Partnership.Tourism NI will support the industry to recruit and retain staff through its ongoing ‘Make it Here’ careers campaign and e-learning platform. In addition, they will work with Fáilte Ireland to extend its Employer Excellence Programme into the north to foster a culture of excellence in the workplace, making tourism and hospitality sectors rewarding and attractive places to work.In partnership with Tourism Ireland, Tourism NI will deliver a significant PR and marketing campaign locally, nationally and globally, in the lead up to, during and after the event. Aside from the world-class golf offering, other experiences across the north will be promoted including high quality accommodation, restaurants, visitor attractions and entertainment. The Tourism NI activity will include a series of golf tour operator and media familiarisations trips. In collaboration with Invest NI, a programme of activity will also be delivered to support the presentation of our unique and compelling inward investment proposition.My vision is to cultivate a productive, regionally balanced and carbon-neutral economy that provides good jobs. The welcome return of The Open to our shores will support that ambition.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the steps being taken to encourage further events at Ebrington Square, Londonderry.
answer_text: The Department is working closely with Derry City and Strabane District Council (DCSDC) and other stakeholders to develop a vibrant and inclusive year-round events programme for Ebrington. A series of community workshops to explore better ways of working together to realise the full potential of Ebrington took place recently. Views expressed at these workshops and those from recent engagement with other stakeholders, will be used to inform the future approach to events and how to maximise the unique space at Ebrington. A call for Expressions of Interest for large scale events in both 2025 and 2026 has recently closed and applications are being assessed by DCSDC at present. The Department has also engaged a local marketing company to market and promote the site and deliver an enhanced animation and events programme through to December 2024 to ensure Ebrington is used and visited by local people across the year. This will include activity during Good Relations Week, Halloween and Christmas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the legislation he intends to bring forward to address the issue of animal cruelty.
answer_text: I have already commenced work on improving animal welfare here by ensuring that the Pet Abduction Act 2024 was extended to Northern Ireland.  I intend to continue to build on this by introducing legislation which will further protect Northern Ireland’s animals, including restrictions on the third-party sale of pups and kittens and the introduction of mandatory CCTV coverage in abattoirs and slaughterhouses.  I intend to meet with stakeholders in the coming weeks to consider what further priority measures can be achieved, within the resources available to my Department, and the time remaining in this mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what steps his Department is taking to reduce waiting lists for the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service in the Western Health and Social Trust.
answer_text: I thank the Member for his question, my Department funded the Youth Wellbeing Prevalence Survey in 2020, which for the first time delivered reliable prevalence estimates of common mental health problems in children and young people in Northern Ireland. The study found that rates of anxiety and depression are around 25% higher in the child and youth population in comparison to other UK nations. These findings helped inform the development of the NI Mental Health Strategy, which had a number of actions specifically aimed at Children and Adolescent Mental Health Services or CAMHS.The Western Health Social Care Trust is reporting recruitment and retention issues along with more complex presentations and both of these factors are having an impact on capacity which is leading to increased waiting times for assessment. Action 32 of The Mental Health Strategy recognises the staffing challenges across CAMHS and a comprehensive workforce review was completed in March 2022. This review considered; existing workforce, the need to adopt new models of working and the importance of increasing the focus on training, recruitment and retention issues. My Department is committed in creating a workforce that will meet the needs of the Western Trust population.The Mental Health Strategy also acknowledged that CAMHS requires additional investment and Action 10 of Mental Health Strategy gives a commitment to increase funding to 10% of the Adult Mental Health Budget. My Department will continue to work closely and collaboratively with the Western Health and Social Care Trust and other key stakeholders to develop services that meet not only current but emerging needs of their population. The challenges of the current health budget position have been recognised and I will continue to work with executive colleagues to ensure CAMHS secure the necessary resources required to meet need.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the next steps for the High Street Task Force.
answer_text: The scope of the High Street Taskforce recommendations span the responsibilities of a number of departments and we are of the view that they should be taken forward by a cross-departmental group of the relevant departments, along with local councils. Therefore, we wrote to Ministerial colleagues at the Department for Communities, Department for the Economy, Department of Finance and Department for Infrastructure on 10th October 2024, asking them to consider the recommendations and determine the way forward, bringing matters to the Executive for consideration as necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps his Department is taking to promote public awareness and understanding of ADHD.
answer_text: The Department does not currently promote public awareness and understanding of Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD). Service provision for assessment and diagnosis of ADHD has grown organically across Trusts in response to population demand and available expertise within Health & Social Care teams. Whilst a level of provision is available for ADHD referrals, there are currently no specifically commissioned services. The Department is aware that this has led to variations in service provision between Trusts and inconsistent pathways. My officials are now considering how best to progress the issue, in discussion with relevant stakeholders. Scoping work is underway to commission research/analysis of ADHD demand to inform options for future commissioning strategies, as well as a regional workshop to explore potential ways forward. This work will be subject to budget availability, but it is hoped that it will inform options for future commissioning strategies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the steps his Department plans to take to compensate farmers for costs incurred by grazing migratory birds.
answer_text: My Department’s current Farm Support and Development Programme is planned to contain similar elements to the Agri-Climate Rural Environment Scheme (ACRES) that is being introduced in Ireland. 		I am always keen to learn from others about interventions that can support both farmers and our rich biodiversity and have asked my officials to liaise with their Irish counterparts as this scheme progresses so that any key learning can be captured and applied as we move forward our farm support programmes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline what steps his Department is taking to address road safety.
answer_text: It is a matter of deep regret that we are continuing to see so many lives lost on our roads. We must remember that 	each life lost is not merely a statistic but family member, a parent, a child, or a friend.	These deaths cause so much pain to families and tear communities apart.	This month I intend to bring a revised Road Safety Strategy to the Executive for its agreement.	This strategy in is an important document to refocus minds and energies on creating safe roads, safe vehicles, and 	safe people. However, it is only the collective efforts of us all that will truly make a difference.	My officials are working alongside those in other Executive Departments, and from emergency services, to ensure	 that we have a single, agreed, and collaborative approach to road safety.	Once the outworkings of the budget are known officials will then work with road safety partners to agree a forward	 action plan – covering engineering, enforcement and education.	Alongside the strategy and action plan I would like to re-emphasise the need for us all, when using our roads and 	our footpaths, to take personal responsibility for behaving in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps his Department will take to address the issue of NHS dental places.
answer_text: A key part of the access issues facing patients were the rates being paid to our dentists for treatment.  The absence of Executive Ministers had meant that it had not been possible to progress the recommended pay uplifts for staffing groups for 2023/24.  In March, I announced a 6% pay uplift for General Dental Services for 2023/24 in line with recommendations contained in the 51st Doctors and Dentists Review Board report. This uplift, which comes with a 7.3% uplift to non-staff expenses for 2023/24, with a 3% recurrent award in future years, will be implemented in the coming weeks.I have also announced a £9.2m investment package for General Dental Services to include:£1m for newly registered child patients through the reestablishment of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme. This scheme provides children aged 0-10 who have not been registered with a dentist with an examination, individualised oral health advice and age-specific fluoride application to teeth to assist with preventing dental decay;£4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for health service fillings, extractions and root canal treatment for 2024/25, to support public access to priority treatments; and£3.9m for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients through a Dental Access Scheme.Until the Dental Access Scheme is implemented, a pilot scheme will continue to run to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group office for advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what guidelines his Department adheres to in relation to the timeframe for directional signage to be installed.
answer_text: My Department does not have specific guidelines in relation to the timeframe for the installation of directional signs as there can be multiple factors including manufacturing, delivery and the availability of land for positioning signs, all of which can influence the time for this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps his Department is taking to support those with learning disabilities to gain employment.
answer_text: My Department takes a range of steps to support those with learning disabilities gain employment, including the provision of inclusive education and skills pathways, support and advice and engagement with other Departments. My Department’s Skills for Life and Work programme provides a stepping stone for participants leaving school with low or no qualifications to access further mainstream training and employment.Typically, around 50% of participants have a disability and to support these young people, the programme has extended age eligibility and allows participants additional time to complete their learning.   Both the Skills for Life and Work and ApprenticeshipsNI programmes include additional support to participants with one or more disability needs, via a separately contracted Disability Support Service.The Level 2 Traineeship and Level 3 Advanced Technical Award programmes are full-time vocational education programmes that are open to anyone over 16 years of age who is not employed.  Both programmes are delivered solely by the Further Education (FE) colleges throughout the region with support for work-based learning provided by local employers.The Traineeship was developed to complement other educational, training and employability initiatives and designed to provide clear progression routes for young people who have completed Entry and Level 1 provision and support an individual’s progression through to higher level studies and employment. The FE colleges have a range of additional support mechanisms for students with learning difficulties and/or disabilities that provide technical and / or personal support. Participants in both programmes have access to all of the supports that are available to students in Further Education Colleges.My Departments Careers Service is an all-age, all-ability service that provides professional and impartial careers services to people in education, employment, training and to the unemployed; including those with a learning disability. This support empowers clients to make their own learning and career decisions, based on up to date labour market information, knowledge of career pathway options and an understanding of their own strengths, talents, skills and interests.I am committed to working with Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities (DLUHC) to ensure clarity for the future and that the best outcome regarding funding and continuity of services can be achieved.I am considering a way forward for skills and employment assistance programmes for the people of the North of Ireland who must navigate the greatest of barriers. I am committed to the social economy and the services they are providing our citizens.In addition, DfE officials participate in a SEN Transitions Working Group which is jointly led by the Departments of Education and Health. The main purpose of this group is to review current transition arrangements for SEN Leavers by gathering evidence, conducting research, considering the remit

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the financial support his Department can provide to health workers who are concerned about car parking charges.
answer_text: As a fair recognition of the hard work and dedication of HSC staff, staff car parking permits became free of charge from 12 May 2024.  There is insufficient capacity for all staff to be provided with an on-site car parking permit.Trusts have a range of supports in place to help encourage a reduction in the demand for onsite staff car parking including free off-site staff parking spaces with free staff shuttle buses to and from work, cycle to work and car share schemes, cycle shelters and showering facilities onsite to encourage cycling, running or walking to work, free evening and weekend parking  passes for parking out of hours, as well as the provision of bespoke Personal Travel Plans for staff. Trusts are also working collaboratively with government departments to provide viable alternatives to single occupancy car use and to increase free off-site staff car parking capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the financial support his Department can provide to smaller schools to expand their outdoor provision.
answer_text: I wrote to all principals on 28 May 2024 advising of my intention to launch a series of targeted smaller scale investment programmes to support and enhance the delivery of the curriculum in schools. One potential programme will be for outdoor play and sports facilities, and this could provide an opportunity for schools to enhance their provision. Further detail on these programmes will be provided in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what projects in the Derry City and Strabane City Deal are under his Department's remit.
answer_text: My Department has been working closely with the Derry City & Strabane District Council on the delivery of the Derry Riverfront development and Strabane Footbridge scheme which are key projects in their city deal. The Riverfront project offers a significant opportunity to reshape public road space along the riverfront, providing improvements to public transport, and enhanced walking and cycling infrastructure. The scheme also provides opportunities for public realm and place-making improvements. My Department is responsible for delivery of the new town centre pedestrian / cycle bridge which will improve connectivity in Strabane and maximise benefits from the wider Strabane Regeneration City Deal Projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps (i) his Department; and (ii) the NIEA are taking to secure the Mobouy site following ongoing fly-tipping.
answer_text: My Department does not own the site and cannot lawfully proceed with additional security measures over and above those already in place. NIEA, an agency of my Department,  has taken all reasonable measures to ensure members of the public do not inadvertently stray onto the site.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he is taking to progress a new Emergency Department for Altnagelvin Hospital.
answer_text: The Western Trust is committed to the prioritisation of a new Emergency Department for Altnagelvin. This is a key priority for the Trust and for the region as the Emergency Department at Altnagelvin is no longer fit for purpose. An initial business case was developed and submitted to Department of Health for consideration as part of their 10-year capital priorities plan. Funding for such a development has not yet been identified. In attempts to reduce the footfall on the Altnagelvin site in the short term, a Minor Injury Unit has been created to provide an alternative pathway away from the main Emergency Department. Approximately 17,000 patients per year will be diverted away from the main ED, therefore enhancing capacity within the main ED and therefore improving the journey for all patients. This Unit is now operational. My Department and the Trust recognises the continued need to develop infrastructure to meet service delivery requirements in modern, safe and fit for purpose buildings and continue to work closely with the Trust to advance the new Emergency Department project in line with the strategic direction of the overall capital plan within health for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the membership of the Ulster University Magee Taskforce.
answer_text: The current agreed membership of the Taskforce is as follows:Chair, Stephen Kelly (CEO, Manufacturing NI)Vice-Chair, Nicola Skelly (Executive Director, Washington Ireland Programme)Professor Paul Bartholomew (Vice-Chancellor, Ulster University)Professor Paul Seawright OBE (Deputy Vice-Chancellor, Ulster University)Moira Doherty (Deputy Secretary, Skills and Education Group, Department for the Economy)John Kelpie (Chief Executive, Derry City & Strabane District Council)Steven Lindsay (Deputy Vice President, Londonderry Chamber of Commerce)Leo Murphy (Principal and Chief Executive, North West Regional College)Paul Hannigan (Head of College, Atlantic Technical University)Dawn McLaughlin (Non-Executive Director, Invest NI)Alison Wallace (Strategy Manager, Waterside Neighbourhood Partnership)Rebecca Allen (President, Ulster University Students Union)Paddy Mackel (University and College Union)Gavin Kileen MBE (Managing Director, Nuprint Technologies Limited)	The membership of the taskforce will be kept under continuous review. Additional participants may be invited to the taskforce where their expertise is relevant to particular topics under discussion.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps his Department is taking to address serious road defects on Woodside Road between Newbuildings and Top of the Hill, Londonderry.
answer_text: As a result of budgetary constraints, my Department has had to introduce a limited service maintenance policy meaning that only the highest priority road defects are repaired. This underfunding has unfortunately resulted in a deteriorating road network.  I can confirm that the steep section of Woodside Road at the Newbuildings end of this route is included within the Department’s resurfacing programme for the Derry City and Strabane District Council area. It is likely that this scheme will be progressed during the 2025/26 year, subject to the availability of funding. The programme for 2025/26 will be developed and published once future budgets have been agreed. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he plans to close the Newbuildings MOT Test Centre if the proposals for a new build facility is approved in the Campsie area.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) is developing proposals for a new test centre at a new DVA site on the McLean Road in the Campsie Industrial Estate, which includes completing detailed designs and obtaining business case approval. Approval to proceed with these proposals will be subject to further consideration and funding availability.The new test centre would provide the capacity to test approximately 85,000 vehicles per annum and, once fully operational, is expected to replace the existing test centre at Newbuildings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is considering introducing Palforzia for allergies in the Western Health and Social Care Trust area.
answer_text: Officers within the Department and its SPPG remain committed to the introduction of a local service to provide Palforzia to patients within Northern Ireland.  Work is ongoing to agree the most appropriate service delivery model for Northern Ireland.  In the interim, Palforzia is available on a case by case basis for eligible patients in line with the Department’s endorsed guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what projects in the Derry City and Strabane City Deal are within his Department's remit.
answer_text: As part of the Derry and Strabane City Deal plans, my Department is the lead Department for the Strabane Town Centre Regeneration Project and the Walled City Regeneration project in Londonderry.  My Department also has an interest in the Central Riverfront project in Londonderry given its regeneration and placemaking elements, but DfC is not expected to be the lead Department for this project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the development of the flood alleviation scheme for Eglinton, Londonderry.
answer_text: Development of flood alleviation proposals for Eglinton are currently being progressed to determine if an economically viable solution can be found. The flooding mechanisms in Eglinton are complex with many compounding factors. A potential solution that would mitigate the risk of flooding from the river has been identified, however, surface water flooding would still affect properties.Therefore, my Department has appointed a consultant to take forward a wider-ranging review to explore potential opportunities to develop more holistic proposals which could provide an acceptable level of protection to homes and businesses in the area from the various sources of flooding. It is hoped this review will be completed by Spring 2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the transfer of Ebrington Square to Derry City and Strabane District Council.
answer_text: TEO officials are working closely with Derry City and Strabane District Council to progress transfer of the whole of the Ebrington site.  More detailed work on future costs is ongoing and options for Council support with some initial elements of site management and maintenance are currently being discussed, with a view to new arrangements being put in place in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what financial support her Department can provide to assist with the provision of CCTV in the Derry City and Strabane District Council area.
answer_text: I fully recognise the outstanding contribution to community safety provided by the CCTV network in the Derry City and Strabane District and the partnership working which is fundamental to its success. Unfortunately, my Department has no specific funding for the provision of CCTV and given the ongoing priorities within the remit of the Department of Justice, and the extraordinary constraints on our budget, that position is unlikely to change in the short term.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what steps their Department plans to take to secure funding for the Fountain Primary School bus through the Urban Villages Programme.
answer_text: Urban Villages has provided funding of £92.6k (ninety-two thousand, six hundred pounds) to the Fountain Primary and Nursery School transport scheme from September 2017 to March 2024.Officials are preparing options on the future of the programme. We look forward to updating Members once we have considered how best to continue with our aim of promoting thriving places.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Rural Development Programme.
answer_text: Although the NI Rural Development Programme has ended, my Department continues to deliver funding to rural communities through a range of interventions including the Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) Programme, the inter-departmental Covid Recovery Small Settlements Regeneration Programme, City and Growth Deals, the Complementary Fund and PEACEPLUS.	As a priority, I am now considering the future of rural policy, including how rural communities can be supported following the ending of the NI Rural Development Programme. It is important to note that responsibility for addressing social and economic issues affecting rural communities rests primarily with the departments which have the statutory remit.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer I gave to AQW 6803/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what steps his Department plans to take to reduce household energy bills.
answer_text: Oral Question No 12 – AQO 186/22-27My department, the Utility Regulator, and the Consumer Council, provides formal regulatory scrutiny of household electricity and natural gas prices. This ensures householders pay fair costs along with a capped profit margin.  The Consumer Council monitors heating oil prices and publishes data on average prices weekly assisting consumers to make informed purchases and encourages competition in the local market.The Energy Strategy aims to stop importing fossil fuels as we become self-sufficient in locally produced renewable energy. The intention is that the renewable energy price will be regulated in similar fashion to the way electricity and gas prices are regulated today.In conclusion, the scope to reduce household energy bills whilst we are importing fossil fuels is very limited.

TNI provides funding to Visit Derry to implement an annual programme of sales and marketing activity supporting local businesses to secure international conferences to the region.TNI provides funding to Visit Derry to deliver a Conference Support Scheme to help off-set the costs to organisers hosting a conference in the region.By working with TNI local businesses can access a range of marketing opportunities such as inclusion on business tourism websites, social media and marketing campaigns.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what support his Department provides to Makaton users, alongside plans to develop sign language legislation.
answer_text: The Department does not provide for the teaching of Makaton and, apart from access to Northern Ireland Registered Intermediary Scheme as part of the The Appeals Service (TAS), it does not provide for any other Makaton support.  The Department of Justice is responsible for delivering the Northern Ireland Registered Intermediary Scheme which provides registered Intermediaries and Communication Specialists to assist tribunal participants with significant communication deficits to communicate more effectively during tribunal proceedings. An established process is in place between DfC and DOJ to access this service. To date there have been no requests for Makaton interpretation services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the feasibility study into Phase 3 of the Londonderry to Coleraine railway line.
answer_text: My Department approved the business case for Phase 3 of the Derry to Coleraine project in November 2022 and issued a letter of offer for £97.7m to take forward this important project for the Northwest. Translink is progressing the project and subject to the availability of funding, construction is scheduled to commence in 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the waiting times for an interpreter service to be available for patients who are hard of hearing, deaf or deaf-blind attending Altnagelvin Hospital outside of normal daytime working hours.
answer_text: The Department of Health commission the contract for the Regional Communication Support Service (RCSS) which is provided by Sign Language Interactions (SLI).  The RCSS is for people who are accessing Health and Social Care services across the whole region, including Out of Hours, for people who are Deaf, Deafblind, or hard of hearing.  The range of interpretation services can be booked for free via a central booking system, by the Health and Social Care Professional / staff.This service is available 24hrs a day, 7 days a week and provides a range of interpreting services, including: in person interpreting services for British Sign Language (BSL) and Irish Sign Language (ISL) users, remote interpreting services (remote: Video Relay Interpreting and Video Interpretation) for British Sign Language (BSL) and Irish Sign Language (ISL) users, interpreters for deafblind people, Lip speakers, Electronic Note takers, and Speech to Text Reporting.The RCSS service is for HSC Northern Ireland primary and secondary care services including Out of Hours.  During out of hours, requests from HSC Staff for interpreters are received by telephone. The out of hours referral is taken immediately by Sign Language Interactions who then make contact with the Interpreters. There are 23 Interpreters available across the region to assist with medical contacts outside of normal working hours.  4 of these primarily cover the Western Health & Social Care Trust area. Any wait time will be from the interpreter accepting the job to arriving at the location.  There is the possibility that there may be an additional wait if all local interpreters are already committed to another referral but this would be the exception rather than the norm.  The option of online remote interpreting is also available to the patient and this option provides instant access with no wait.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what preparations and preventative measures his Department has in place following the first confirmed case of measles diagnosed in Northern Ireland in seven years.
answer_text: Measles is a highly contagious, serious viral infection with complications including meningitis and hearing loss. It is the most infectious of all diseases transmitted through the respiratory route. Measles is a notifiable disease under the Public Health Act (Northern Ireland) 1967 and should be reported by clinicians to the Public Health Agency (PHA) when a case is suspected based on clinical presentation.The most effective measure to stop measles cases emerging, and to contain wider spread of imported cases, is to ensure that 95% of the population is vaccinated with two doses of the Measles, Mumps and Rubella (MMR) vaccine. Due to falling uptake of the MMR vaccine over several years, an MMR vaccine catchup campaign has been launched locally. Individuals up to 25 years of age, who have had less than two doses of a measles containing vaccine, are being contacted and invited to receive the MMR vaccine via GP surgeries and Trust vaccination clinics. Those aged 26 years of age and over who remain unvaccinated or partially unvaccinated can request the vaccine from their GP practice. Over 32,000 letters have now been issued to children and young people who have been identified as missing either one or both MMR vaccination. The letters include information about where to find details of local vaccine clinics. The letters also have a telephone number and PHA have set up a helpline which is running from 5th February – 31st March 2024.  This helpline is providing further advice and information about the vaccination and where to get vaccinated. The Public Health Agency (PHA) is leading the overall multi-agency response to manage measles cases in Northern Ireland. A PHA chaired multi stakeholder working group has been established to coordinate readiness plans from the public health perspective and to ensure readiness in terms of the outbreak management response, laboratory testing arrangements and infection prevention control arrangements.A communication entitled ‘Preventing a Measles Resurgence in Northern Ireland’ was circulated by the Director of Public Health in PHA to Trust Medical Directors, Directors of Nursing, Primary Care GPs, Community Pharmacists and Out of Hours GPs on 26 January 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps he is taking to (i) alleviate pressures on DfI Roads divisions; and (ii) reduce the standard response time for the maintenance of roads and defects of between 4 to 6 weeks.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a very difficult financial position due to years of underfunding that has been compounded by recent inflationary pressures and the absence of the Assembly. That has had a significant impact on the Department’s ability to deliver essential maintenance functions.Recruitment and retention of staff is also challenging, meaning that the part of the Department responsible for managing the road network currently has a higher than acceptable vacancy rate. My Department is taking steps to deliver a programme of recruitment and training opportunities, particularly focused on those roles within the technical and operational areas of the organisation to deliver capacity and capability.  This will include direct entrant competitions for recently qualified graduates, and apprenticeship opportunities to encourage pathways to a career in civil and infrastructure engineering.While my officials and maintenance teams have worked very hard to maintain the road network, they have, nonetheless, been forced to concentrate the limited resources available on the highest-priority repairs. The level of repairs that can be carried out is dependent on the budget determination for my Department, and until such times as sufficient funding is made available, it will not be possible to change the current level of service. I will continue to raise this matter with my Executive colleagues as more funding is needed to change the level of road maintenance in the long term. However, I recently announced an additional £1m of funding to address potholes and £8.1m of funding for structural maintenance to alleviate some of the pressures faced by my Department

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department has taken to ensure that drinking water is not affected by the Mobuoy waste site.
answer_text: DAERA works collaboratively with NI Water to safeguard the drinking water supplied from Carmoney Water Treatment Works (WTW) for the protection of public health.My officials have put in place a comprehensive environmental monitoring programme which includes: site inspections; regular monitoring of ground and surface waters; and daily laboratory testing of water quality at the NI Water drinking water abstraction point. NI Water also monitors the quality of water abstracted from the River Faughan at the Carmoney WTW. At the works, raw water is subject to treatment and the effectiveness of this is confirmed by samples taken of the works final water, at Service Reservoirs in the distribution system, and at consumer taps.  The Drinking Water Inspectorate, a unit within NIEA, is responsible for assessing these results against The Water Supply (Water Quality) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017.Monitoring data is shared and interpreted by both NIEA and NI Water to continually assess any changes in risk to the water quality in the River Faughan, and at the NI Water abstraction point. NI Water has tested procedures in place to ensure that, if the need did arise, it would immediately stop taking water from the River Faughan. In an emergency, supplies can be provided from other treatment works. Water Quality reports are available on the DAERA website. To date, there has been no adverse impact reported on the safety of the drinking water supplied from Carmoney WTW.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what interpretive services are available for those who are hard of hearing, deaf or deaf-blind attending Altnagelvin Hospital Emergency Department and Out of Hours.
answer_text: The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) commission the contract for the Regional Communication Support Service (RCSS) which is provided by Sign Language Interactions (SLI).  The RCSS is for people who are accessing Health and Social Care services across the whole region, including Emergency Departments and Out of Hours, for people who are Deaf, Deafblind, or hard of hearing.  The range of interpretation services can be booked for free via a central booking system, by the Health and Social Care Professional / staff.This service is available 24hrs a day, 7 days a week and provides a range of interpreting services, including: in person interpreting services for British Sign Language (BSL) and Irish Sign Language (ISL) users, remote interpreting services (remote: Video Relay Interpreting and Video Interpretation) for British Sign Language (BSL) and Irish Sign Language (ISL) users, interpreters for deafblind people, Lipspeakers, Electronic Note takers, and Speech to Text Reporting.The RCSS service is for HSC Northern Ireland primary and secondary care services including Emergency Departments and Out of Hours.  During out of hours, requests from HSC Staff for interpreters are received by telephone.  Interpreters are then contacted accordingly.  The option of online remote interpreting is also available to the patient.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps his Department is taking to develop rail connectivity from Londonderry and the North West.
answer_text: My Department approved the business case for Phase 3 of the Derry to Coleraine project in November 2022 and issued a letter of offer for £97.7m to take forward this important project for the Northwest. Translink are now progressing with the project. The exact timeline of the various phases of the project is subject to the outcome of the capital budget process and the availability of funding in future years.  A study to examine the possibility of additional rail halts on the Coleraine to Derry line forms part of the feasibility study that is also examining the introduction of a half hourly service between Belfast and Derry. This study is currently ongoing and is planned to be completed later this year. In addition, the All-Island Strategic Rail Review (AISRR) includes a number of recommendations in relation to Derry and the Northwest, including a dual-tracked, electrified line between Portadown and Derry, which would link Strabane, Omagh, and Dungannon to the rail network and greatly improve intercity connectivity between Derry, Dublin and Belfast; a new spur from Derry to Letterkenny; and consideration to be given to new halts in Derry, including at Limavady.Following closure of the AISRR public consultation in September 2023, work is ongoing to finalise the Review and its recommendations. Once the Review is published, further work will be required to prioritise the recommendations and then to undertake feasibility studies and develop business cases to support future investment decisions, including those in relation to the development of rail connectivity to Derry and the Northwest.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when the review of Higher Education funding will be completed.
answer_text: I recognise the role of Higher Education in a thriving, regionally balanced economy; however, it is clear that there are significant financial pressures facing both the institutions and their current and prospective students.  I have asked my officials to update me on any work to date on a review of Higher Education Funding, as well as proposals to take it forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any discussions have taken place between his Department and Derry City and Strabane District Council to progress a walkway and cycle path between Newbuildings and Bready.
answer_text: Officials in my Department are working closely with councils, including Derry City and Strabane District Council, to better understand their 5-year greenway programme, and to develop an appropriate grant and support framework for the effective delivery of such programmes.The route between Newbuildings and Bready is being considered as part of this process, and officials will continue to collaborate with councils on the development of greenways and active travel routes.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) what their Department, through Urban Villages, paid for the Meenan Square site, Londonderry; and (ii) to detail the Land and Property Services valuation for the site.
answer_text: Apex Housing Association Ltd, funded by The Executive Office, purchased the majority of the site from the private sector landowner Westco Developments in March 2023.The amount granted by the department to Apex Housing Association Ltd. in relation to the purchase of Meenan Square was £1,004,300.Site acquisitions funded by The Executive Office are subject to a full valuation by Land and Property Services. TEO and Apex Housing Association used the LPS valuation to determine the purchase price for the proportion of the Meenan Square site to be acquired. LPS provided the valuation report to TEO in accordance with their standard terms and conditions. Valuations are held by LPS in confidence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what action his Department is taking to implement the strategic actions arising from the (i) Independent Review of Invest NI; and (ii) subsequent action plan.
answer_text: My department published a co-designed Action Plan which sets out 35 Strategic Actions to address the recommendations from the Independent Review. Immediate action has been taken to stabilise Invest NI’s leadership team.  A permanent Chief Executive Officer has been appointed to lead the agency, and a new Chair of the Invest NI Board will assume position from 1st March 2024. My department chairs a joint Implementation Oversight Group which oversees the work required to deliver the remaining Strategic Actions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the Derry and Strabane City Deal.
answer_text: City and Growth Deals are long-term capital investment programmes. There is a significant amount of work to be done by the various stakeholders between Heads of Terms and signing the Deal which marks the stage that funding can be released.The Derry City and Strabane District Council City Deal is nearing the end of the business case process and is working towards signing the Deal in spring/ summer this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the progress of Phase 2 of the the A6 Dualling Scheme from Drumahoe to Gransha, Londonderry.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety. Part of the road alignment for Phase 2 of the A6 encroaches on the Mobuoy waste site; Once plans for remediation of that site are in place, a delivery strategy for Phase 2 including timelines will be finalised.Details of the current status of Phase 2 of the A6 project will continue to be available on the project webpage which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/a6-londonderry-dungiven-overview

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what measures his Department is taking to address the ongoing traffic concerns at Caw Roundabout, Londonderry.
answer_text: The upgrade and signalisation of the Caw Roundabout is a planning condition of the development at H30, which is to be constructed prior to the occupation of the 301st dwelling. This upgrade is required to provide mitigation against the increased traffic flow resulting from construction of the new housing development.In addition to this upgrade, my Department is considering further enhancements to the roundabout which would also improve overall safety and congestion issues at the site.Ideally it would be beneficial to deliver both the H30 and Department upgrades as a single project, to minimise disruption to road users.  However, given the significant funding pressures currently faced by my Department, there is currently no funding available to complete the proposed DfI upgrades and my consideration of future funding for such works will be informed by the transport priorities identified by the Regional Strategic Transport Network Transport Plan.  Therefore, the delivery of the H30 upgrade proposals at Caw Roundabout as a separate project currently seems the most viable option.Discussions are ongoing between departmental officials and the developer to agree the timeframe for the delivery of the H30 upgrade to Caw Roundabout.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what progress has been made on the flood alleviation scheme for the Ivy Mead, Drumahoe.
answer_text: Development of potential flood alleviation proposals for Drumahoe remains ongoing. An economically viable scheme has been identified and consultants have been instructed to commence the design phase of this project. Progress to procurement will be dependent on proposals remaining viable and availability of capital funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what support his Department can provide to bring the All-Ireland Pipe Band Championships back to Londonderry.
answer_text: The All-Ireland Pipe Band Championship is a joint initiative between The Royal Scottish Pipe Band Association NI Branch and the Irish Pipe Band Association and is an important event in the banding calendar.   Decisions on the venue are a matter for the organisers.The Arts Council of Northern Ireland is the Department’s principal means for the delivery of support to the arts.  The Arts Council supports the Royal Scottish Pipe Band Association NI Branch through its Annual Funding Programme, providing funding towards the Pipe Band College and core costs.  I have no doubt that Londonderry would be as welcoming a host for the Championship, as Lurgan and Ballymena have been in recent years; however the Arts Council does not sponsor or provide funding towards the cost of competitions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline a timeframe for the completion of the A6 road upgrade.
answer_text: The pandemic has had a major impact on delivery of the A6 scheme with various activities having been disrupted due to social distancing requirements, staff absences and difficulties with the supply chain. Current market volatility is also impacting on delivery timescales.Despite these exceptional challenges, my Department and our contractors have sought to maintain progress on this strategically important road scheme. Although the scheme is now well advanced, a significant amount of work has still to be completed before it can be fully opened, which includes the provision of safety barrier, road marking, signage, street lighting and the completion of a Road Safety Audit.My officials are continuing to work closely with the contractor with the aim of having this work completed as quickly as practicable so that the road can be opened in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps her Department is taking to promote safe swimming and water safety in the education sector.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland curriculum provides opportunities for young people to learn about the importance of personal safety across all Key Stages, including the importance of safety in a number of familiar and unfamiliar environments, which includes safety in the water.  At primary level these issues are addressed through the Personal Development and Mutual Understanding area of learning.  At post-primary level, they are addressed through the Learning for Life and Work area of learning.  Pupils also have the opportunity to pursue qualifications at Key Stage 4 and post-16 that explicitly cover issues relevant to personal safety.Furthermore, swimming is a statutory area of the curriculum within the Physical Education (PE) area of learning.  At Key Stage 2, pupils can develop basic swimming and personal survival skills, and progress from using a swimming aid to developing their confidence and competence in being able to swim without the use of any aids using recognised swimming strokes.  At Key Stage 3, in PE lessons, pupils have opportunities to build on and increase their knowledge, understanding and skills through frequent and regular participation in a balanced programme, including in swimming.  My officials regularly share important messages to schools via the C2K network.  In the past, this has included a reminder to young people about the dangers of freshwater swimming.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps his Department is taking to address flooding concerns in Ivy Mead, Waterside area, Londonderry.
answer_text: The issue of flooding in Drumahoe is complex with many compounding factors. My Department is continuing work to determine if an economically viable solution can be found that would provide the recommended standard of flood protection considered necessary for a flood alleviation scheme. Since completion of the feasibility study, which was commissioned as a result of the 2017 floods, significant progress has been made and the proposed scheme is now at the design stage. Work completed includes, the appointment of a consultant to progress the design of the scheme, completion of extensive ground investigations, detailed design works, an outline environmental screening assessment an EIA scoping exercise, topographical surveys, additional river modelling and further optioneering. My Department will be reviewing our current proposals in light of the recent flooding to ensure that they would be effective in reducing the impact of any river flooding to properties in Drumahoe. Subject to being able to demonstrate the economic viability and affordability of any proposals and gaining the required approvals my Department will progress the scheme as promptly as possible.My Department also administers the Homeowner Flood Protection Grant Scheme (HFPGS).  This scheme is designed to encourage the owners of residential properties that are located within known flood prone areas to modify their properties to make them more resistant to flooding. NI Water has also advised that there is an issue in the area regarding excess storm water entering the foul sewers and, in periods of extremely high flow, this causes them to exceed their capacity.  Before a long-term solution can be identified, NI Water has indicated that it needs to undertake further wastewater flow surveys. It is aiming to have this monitoring and sewer flow modelling work completed by the end of 2022 with a view to confirming a long-term solution in early 2023.NI Water is also aware of concerns about the neighbouring development at Birch Hill and is continuing to investigate matters there and how they may be contributing to the above-outlined issues, and the flooding which has affected Ivy Mead Mews.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what support her Department is providing to ensure the sustainability of rural schools.
answer_text: All grant-aided schools are funded under the Common Funding Scheme (CFS).  The Common Funding Formula (CFF) is the Department’s mechanism for allocating budgets to schools funded through Local Management of Schools (LMS) arrangements.  A school’s CFF allocation is determined by a combination of its overall enrolment, the differentiated needs of its pupils and the school’s characteristics, with one such characteristic being the size of the school.  Two such factors are the Small Schools Support Factor (SSSF), which provides the following:-Primary Schools Small School Support Factor:  A lump sum equivalent to 1.0 times the salary (inclusive of employers National Insurance and Pension contributions) of a teacher on point UPS 1 of the Teachers’ Upper Pay Scale at the rate as at the census date on which funding is calculated, will be allocated to schools of up to 100 pupils tapering to zero at 300 pupils.  For 2022-23 the initial lump sum is £51,863.Primary Principal Release Factors:  This factor is intended to provide resources to help ensure that at least two days per week release from teaching duties is available for Principals of smaller primary schools.  Primary schools with enrolments of up to 100 pupils will receive a maximum lump sum amount tapering to zero at 300 pupils.  For 2022-23, the initial lump sum is £16,420.  Post Primary Schools:  A lump sum equivalent to 3.4 times the salary (inclusive of employers National Insurance and Pension contributions) of a teacher on point UPS 1 of the Teachers’ Upper Pay Scale at the rate as at the census date on which funding is calculated, will be allocated to schools of up to 200 pupils, tapering to zero at 550 pupils.  For 2022-23 the initial lump sum is £176,334.That said, CFF factor funding which relates to school size is provided regardless of the location of the school setting as the additional allocation of funding is to support the delivery of the curriculum.  These factors are of equal benefit to all smaller schools regardless of whether they are a smaller rural or indeed a smaller urban school or a developing school (eg newly established Irish Medium (IM) or Grant Maintained Integrated (GMI) school).  From an Area Planning perspective, the education of our children and young people in educationally and financially viable and sustainable schools with access to the full curriculum is a critical issue regardless of where they live.  As the majority of small schools are in rural areas the Department is supporting them through the allocation of the SSSF.My Department’s Sustainable Schools Policy (SSP), which is the cornerstone of the Area Planning process, recognises the challenges facing rural schools through, for example, a lower enrolment threshold for primary schools in those settings. I acknowledge the need for sustainable education provision in rural areas and have tasked my officials to consider and develop a related programme. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what support her Department plans to provide for the 2022 Foyle Cup.
answer_text: My Department plans to provide funding of £16,000 towards the 2022 Foyle Cup.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline when the current phase of the A6 upgrade at Drumahoe will be completed.
answer_text: The pandemic has had a major impact on delivery of the scheme with various activities being disrupted due to social distancing requirements, staff absences and difficulties with the supply chain. Current market volatility is also impacting delivery.My Department and our contractors have sought to maintain progress on this strategically important road scheme despite the exceptional challenges. Completion is now scheduled for later this year.My officials continue to work closely with the contractor to agree a likely date for opening.
